true_relatives.meaning: is (third-person singular of as, to be)
true_relatives.note: Sanskrit asti is the direct ancestor of Hindi है — both mean 'is', from the same PIE root for to be
English 'is' and Sanskrit asti (ancestor of है) both descend from PIE *h₁es-ti — they are true cognates; hai and 'is' are the same ancient word in different clothes
Russian есть shares the same PIE *h₁es- root as है — both are third-person singular present-tense forms of 'to be'

useful_coincidences.note: Hebrew יש (yesh) expresses existence — the same philosophical function as है, which asserts that something is or exists

sound_alikes.note: है sounds like a slightly prolonged 'hi' — imagine greeting someone and pointing at them: 'hi, you IS here!'
'hay' (dried grass) rhymes perfectly with है — picture a hay bale that simply IS there, existing in the field

evolution.meaning: is (3rd singular present; also 2nd intimate singular)

root_breakdown.logic: The PIE third-person 'is' compressed through Sanskrit asti → Prakrit atthi → Hindi है, shedding all but the essential 'h' breath and the 'ai' vowel — one of the most ancient words in any language, stripped to its barest form.

etymology_story: है shares its ancestry with English 'is', Russian есть, and Latin est — all descend from the Proto-Indo-European *h₁es-ti, meaning 'is'.
Sanskrit preserved this as asti, a full two-syllable word used extensively in texts.
Through Prakrit and Apabhraṃśa it wore down to the single syllable hai.
In Hindi, है is used with यह, वह (singular things and people), and with तू (intimate second-person) — the workhorse of the present tense.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable — a light 'h' followed by the 'ai' diphthong, similar to saying 'hi' but with a fuller 'ai' at the end

tips.tip: है sounds exactly like English 'hay' — same vowel, same light h at the start

memory_hook.primary: A single hay bale sitting in an empty golden field — solid, present, undeniably there
memory_hook.connection: The hay bale simply IS there — it exists, it is present — capturing the core meaning of है as the most basic assertion of being

true_relatives.note: Hindi हो descends from the Sanskrit root bhū (to be, to become) via Prakrit — a different Sanskrit root from asti, giving a distinct set of conjugated forms

useful_coincidences.note: English 'ho!' (a call to attention) is spelled identically — imagine calling out 'ho!' to a friend informally, the way you'd use तुम and हो
The Russian laughter syllable хо has the same sound — light, casual, friendly — matching the informal register of हो

sound_alikes.note: Identical in spelling and sound to English exclamation 'ho' — a casual, friendly call that matches the informal register of हो perfectly

root.meaning: to be, to become, to exist

evolution.meaning: are (with तुम); also used in subjunctive

root_breakdown.logic: Sanskrit bhū (to become) gave a parallel set of 'to be' forms alongside asti/है — these bhū-derived forms handle the informal second person and the subjunctive mood in Hindi.

etymology_story: हो comes from the Sanskrit root bhū, meaning to be or to become — a slightly more dynamic root than the pure asti ('to be' in a static sense).
Sanskrit bhavasi ('you are') compressed through Prakrit hosi to the single syllable ho.
In Hindi, हो is used specifically with तुम — the informal second person — making it the casual, everyday 'you are'.
The same form हो also appears in the subjunctive mood, expressing wishes, possibilities, and conditions.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable — a rounded, open 'o' sound with a clear 'h' breath at the start

tips.tip: sounds exactly like the English exclamation 'ho' — same vowel, same consonant

memory_hook.primary: Two friends at a crossroads, one casually raising a hand to the other with a relaxed wave
memory_hook.connection: The relaxed, casual wave between friends captures the informal register of हो — used with तुम, among people who are comfortable with each other but not intimately close

examples.hindi: जो भी हो, हम साथ हैं।
examples.romanisation: jo bhī ho, ham sāth haĩ.

words.english: are (to be — formal 2nd person / plural 3rd person)

true_relatives.meaning: they are (plural third-person of as, to be)
true_relatives.note: Sanskrit santi is the plural ancestor of Hindi हैं — both are the plural/formal present of 'to be'
English 'are' fills the same grammatical slot as Hindi हैं — the plural or formal second-person present tense of 'to be'

useful_coincidences.note: Hebrew הן (hen) means 'they are' — the same plural third-person function as Hindi हैं when used with वे

sound_alikes.note: हैं and है differ only in nasality — है (hai) for singular intimate, हैं (haĩ) for plural and formal; the nasal hum signals plurality and formality
The nasal ending of हैं rhymes loosely with 'hayne' or 'hain' — the buzzing nasal is the key distinguishing feature from है

root_breakdown.logic: The plural of 'to be' from PIE compressed into Hindi हैं — the nasal ending (the anusvara ँ) is the sonic marker of plurality, carrying the sense of 'more than one' or 'formal address' in a single nasal buzz.

etymology_story: हैं shares its roots with Sanskrit santi (they are) and ultimately with PIE *h₁s-énti, the plural of 'to be'.
Through Prakrit and Apabhraṃśa, the form compressed into the single nasal syllable haĩ.
The nasal ending (written as ँ in Devanagari) is the crucial marker — it distinguishes हैं (plural/formal) from है (singular/intimate).
In Hindi, हैं is used with आप (formal you), हम (we), ये (these/they, near), and वे (those/they, remote).

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable — like है (hay) but with the vowel resonating through the nose at the end; the nasal hum is the key distinguishing feature

tips.tip: start with the 'hay' sound as in है, then let it resonate nasally through your nose — that nasal hum at the end is हैं

memory_hook.primary: A crowd of people all standing together, each with a small humming tuning fork held up, all vibrating in unison
memory_hook.connection: The shared nasal vibration of the crowd captures the nasal ending of हैं — that buzz in the nose marks plurality and formality, the thing that separates हैं from है

true_relatives.meaning: was, became (past of bhū, to be)
true_relatives.note: Hindi था descends from the Sanskrit past forms of bhū (to be/become) — the same root that gives होना and all the ho- forms

useful_coincidences.note: Russian тот (that, masculine) begins with 'то' — sounds like the start of था, and like था it is a masculine form; helps fix the masculine gender agreement of था
था sounds like 'thaw' — the past melting away like था, which refers to what has already gone

sound_alikes.note: 'thaw' rhymes with था — like how the past thaws away and is gone, था marks what was but is no more

root_breakdown.logic: The Sanskrit past tense of bhū (to be) evolved through Prakrit into the था/थी/थे/थीं paradigm — a fully gender-agreeing past tense unique to the 'to be' verb in Hindi.

etymology_story: था comes from Sanskrit bhū (to be/become), specifically its past forms, which expressed a state that existed at a prior time.
Through Prakrit and Apabhraṃśa the past forms developed the distinctive 'th' initial consonant still present today.
In Hindi, था always agrees with the grammatical gender and number of the subject: था for masculine singular, थी for feminine singular.
This gender agreement of the past tense of होना is one of Hindi's most important grammatical features.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable — the 'th' is aspirated (a puff of air follows the t), then a long open 'aa' vowel

tips.tip: था sounds very much like English 'thaw' — the same aspirated 'th' sound at the start and a similar open vowel

memory_hook.primary: A melting ice sculpture of a man — solid and present in the past, now slowly becoming water and disappearing
memory_hook.connection: The masculine figure of ice that WAS solid but is now melting captures था — the masculine singular past, something that existed but is now gone

true_relatives.note: The same Sanskrit root bhū that gave था (masculine) also gave थी (feminine) — the suffix -ī marks feminine in the past tense across Hindi

useful_coincidences.note: थी sounds like archaic English 'thee' — both are small, intimate words; the 'thee/thī' pairing can help fix the feminine past 'was'
Russian была (byla) is the feminine past form of 'to be' — exactly the same grammatical function as थी; both languages mark feminine past distinctly

sound_alikes.note: थी sounds like archaic English 'thee' — imagine 'thee was here' as an old-fashioned feminine past, anchoring the feminine and past meanings together
थी sounds exactly like 'tea' — picture a woman's tea cup that WAS here but is now empty and gone

root_breakdown.logic: The feminine suffix -ī is systematic in Hindi past tense — just as लड़की is feminine लड़का, थी is the feminine counterpart to था; the -ī ending is the universal feminine marker in Hindi's past tense system.

etymology_story: थी shares its root with था — both come from Sanskrit bhū (to be) — but carries the feminine suffix -ī that marks the speaker or subject as feminine.
This gender agreement in past tense is a defining feature of Hindi grammar, absent from English but present in Russian (была vs. был).
A woman saying 'I was fine' says मैं ठीक थी — the थी tells us immediately that the speaker is female.
Likewise, referring to a girl or any feminine noun in the past requires थी — the word itself encodes both past tense and feminine gender.

pronunciation_guide.stress: single syllable — aspirated 'th' at the start, then a long 'ee' vowel

tips.tip: थी sounds exactly like English 'tea' — same long 'ee' vowel, the 'th' is just slightly more aspirated than the English 't'

memory_hook.primary: An empty tea cup left on a table, with a faint impression of lipstick on the rim — someone feminine was here, but is now gone
memory_hook.connection: The empty tea cup and the lipstick trace encode both the feminine gender and the past tense of थी — she WAS here, now she is gone
